_note: Official source pages watched for change (new compilation, remake, new amending instrument, or revised standard). Monitoring only detects and queues; a maintainer reviews every flag and decides what to ingest. Nothing is ingested automatically. last_sha256 is set on the first successful baseline run.

sources.name: AML/CTF Act 2006 — compilations (C2006A00169)
sources._note: 2026-07-21: the Federal Register of Legislation exposes a PUBLIC OData API at api.prod.legislation.gov.au/v1 requiring no key. Monitoring the Versions entity set reports the compilation NUMBER and its start date, so a change reads 'Compilation 62 from 2026-07-01' instead of 'the page differs'. URL is stored percent-encoded because OData filters contain spaces, which urllib rejects. This replaces an unattended Cowork scheduled task that could never work: it used WebFetch, which requires interactive permission approval, so every unattended run failed with a permission timeout. VERIFIED 2026-07-21: the endpoint returns Compilation No. 62 (start 2026-07-01, registered 2026-07-10), superseding the No. 61 baseline — exactly the change that task was asked to watch for.

sources.name: Charter of the United Nations Act 1945 (C1945A00032)
UK Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (c. 29) — revised
ignore_patterns: is up to date with all changes known to be in force on or before \d{1,2} \w+ \d{4}
sources._note: 2026-07-21: legislation.gov.uk revised pages stamp TODAY'S DATE into the body — "is up to date with all changes known to be in force on or before <date>" — so the page hash changed every day and this source flagged CHANGED on every run. VERIFIED by fetching the live page: the banner read the current date. The banner is excluded from hashing via ignore_patterns; nothing else is excluded, and a substantive amendment still flags (asserted in the monitor selftest). BETTER FIX LATER: legislation.gov.uk publishes an official API — moving to monitor_url + json_extract would track amendment identifiers structurally instead of excluding a fragment.

sources.name: US Bank Secrecy Act — 31 U.S.C. ch. 53 subch. II (current)
US 31 C.F.R. Part 1010 — FinCEN general provisions (eCFR)

json_extract._note: FinCEN's general provisions run to 157 sections. Record-level monitoring names which section was amended and when, rather than reporting that a 157-section payload differs.

sources._note: 2026-07-18: the human-facing eCFR page BLOCKS automated fetches — it redirected to unblock.federalregister.gov ('Request Access'), so this source was hashing an access-denied page, not FinCEN's rules. Switched to the OFFICIAL eCFR versioner API, published for programmatic use (no stealth, no proxy). It reports amendment dates per SECTION, so a change names the sections amended. The identical defect and identical fix were found in the deep-seabed corpus the same day (15 CFR 970/971). Hash cleared to force a fresh baseline.

sources.name: UN Convention against Transnational Organized Crime (Palermo/UNTOC 2000, UNODC)
UN International Convention for the Suppression of the Financing of Terrorism (1999, UNODC)
UN Security Council Resolution 1267 (1999)
sources._note: 2026-07-18: undocs.org now redirects to docs.un.org; pointed at the canonical host rather than relying on a redirect that may not persist. NOTE: last_sha256 was already null — the monitor has never successfully baselined this or the other two resolutions, so they have been listed but effectively unwatched. Likely needs MANUAL classification (document viewer, not plain HTML); confirm before assuming the URL change alone fixes it.

sources.name: Canada — Special Economic Measures Act (S.C. 1992, c. 17)
Canada — United Nations Act (R.S.C. 1985, c. U-2)
Singapore — Precious Stones and Precious Metals Act 2019 (Act 7 of 2019)
Canada — Freezing Assets of Corrupt Foreign Officials Act (S.C. 2011, c. 10)
Hong Kong — Cross-boundary Currency & BNI Ordinance (Cap. 629)
Japan — Criminal Proceeds Transfer Prevention Act (Act 22/2007)

_pending_once_ids_confirmed: AUSTRAC sector/regulatory guidance pages (guidance, not law — out of scope unless promoted)
_reliability: Some official portals are JavaScript single-page apps: a stdlib fetch sees a shell, not the law, so a naive hash would report 'unchanged' forever. Such sources carry "render":"spa" and are reported MANUAL (checked by hand each cycle) unless a "monitor_url" points at a server-rendered artifact that is fetched instead. Japan uses the e-Gov XML API. Hong Kong e-Legislation, the UAE portal, and Swiss Fedlex have no stdlib-reachable text artifact and are manual-review. watch_sources.py also flags any fetch that returns too little text (SUSPECT).
